# Cross-Backend Test Fixtures

Deterministic input tensors and expected outputs shared across all
backends of the cross-language correctness matrix.

## Why fixtures (not in-memory generation per backend)

The matrix verifies that the SAME kernel produces the SAME output when
dispatched from different host languages (Python, C, Rust, Prolog).
For this comparison to mean anything, every backend must dispatch the
kernel on IDENTICAL inputs. RNGs differ across languages — `np.random`
vs `torch.randn` vs `rand_chacha` vs Prolog's `random/1` all produce
different sequences even with the same seed.

Solution: generate the inputs ONCE in Python, persist as `.npy`, every
backend reads the same file.

## File format

NumPy `.npy` format (NEP 1, stable, self-describing). Each file contains
exactly one ndarray: dtype, shape, and binary data in one stream.

Tools per language:
  Python: `np.save(path, array)`, `np.load(path)`
  C: ~30 lines parsing/writing the header (see harness_npy.c when shipped)
  Rust: `npyz` crate
  Prolog: small read_binary_file + header parser (or via library if available)

## Naming convention

Inputs: `<op_kind>_<shape>.npy` — shared across all cells of a kernel.

Outputs: `<op_kind>_<shape>_cell<N>_<lang>_<dev>.npy` — names the specific
cell of the 7-cell matrix (per mavchin's framing, 2026-05-17 21:42 UTC).

The 7 cells of the matrix per kernel:
  [1] C CPU              — C host, native CPU arithmetic
  [2] C GPU              — C host, CUDA kernel
  [3] Python CPU         — Python host, PyTorch/NumPy reference
  [4] Python GPU         — Python host, ctypes → CUDA kernel
  [5] Rust CPU           — Rust host, native f32 arithmetic
  [6] Rust/cudarc GPU    — Rust host, cudarc → CUDA kernel
  [7] (N/A)              — cuda-oxide is GPU-only
  [8] Rust/cuda-oxide GPU — Rust host, pure-Rust PTX kernel

Examples:
  `ggml_sum_rows_8x16.npy`                       — shared input, [8, 16] float32
  `ggml_sum_rows_8x16_cell3_python_cpu.npy`      — cell [3] output, [8] float32
  (cell [2] outputs are written to /tmp/ by the C harness at run time;
   not stored in this directory since they're regenerated per build)

When a cell's output stabilizes and we want it pinned in git as a
canonical bit-identical reference (e.g., once mavchin's bit-identical
spike produces Ollama-pinned outputs), it will land here with the
naming above. Until then, runtime cells use /tmp/ paths.

### Why this naming over `_expected.npy`

Pre-21:42-UTC convention used `_expected.npy` for the PyTorch reference,
implicitly treating it as the ground truth. But the matrix's substantive
claim is that NO single backend is privileged ground truth — each cell
is a peer. The renamed convention makes cell membership structural:
`_cell3_python_cpu.npy` names what the file IS, not what it does for us.

## Versioning

Fixtures are version-controlled. Bit-identical regeneration is possible
via `tests/fixtures/regenerate.py` for reproducibility. Do not regenerate
unless intentionally changing the test corpus.

Each fixture is small (kilobytes); storing them in git is the substrate-
honest choice — every commit pins the exact input each backend saw.

## Tolerance

When comparing backend outputs to the reference fixture:
  rtol=1e-5, atol=1e-6 (standard fp32, matching cpu_references.py)

Bit-identical equality is the GPU↔reference goal but not guaranteed
because fp32 reduction order matters. Numerical-close (allclose) is the
practical contract.

## Initial fixtures (1.c.i, 2026-05-17)

Generated by regenerate.py with `torch.manual_seed(42)`:

  ggml_sum_rows_8x16.npy                       — [8, 16] float32, random
  ggml_sum_rows_8x16_cell3_python_cpu.npy      — [8] float32, sum over axis=1
